questions.question: What is globalisation?
questions.answer: Globalisation is the process of rapid integration and interconnection between countries through foreign trade and foreign investments by multinational corporations (MNCs). It creates global production and market networks.
questions.solution: Globalisation involves countries becoming economically integrated through: (1) MNCs establishing production facilities across multiple countries, (2) Trade in goods and services expanding across borders, (3) Investment flowing from wealthy to developing countries. Unlike historical trade in completed goods, modern globalisation fragments production—components are made in different countries, assembled elsewhere, and sold globally. This creates interdependence between nations and rapid spread of technology, capital, and ideas.

questions.question: What is a Multinational Corporation (MNC)?
questions.answer: An MNC is a company that owns or controls production facilities in more than one nation. MNCs establish offices and factories in multiple countries to reduce costs and maximize profits.
questions.solution: MNCs are the primary agents of globalisation. They operate in dozens of countries, moving capital and technology globally. MNCs set up production in regions with cheap labour and resources, keeping costs low. Example: An equipment MNC might design in USA, manufacture components in China, assemble in Mexico, provide customer service in India, and sell globally. This global spread of MNCs is the defining characteristic of modern globalisation.

questions.question: Name three ways MNCs control global production.
questions.answer: Setting up own factories and offices; buying and integrating local companies; placing orders with small local producers, controlling prices and conditions.
questions.solution: MNCs control production through direct investment (building factories), acquisition (buying existing companies), and outsourcing (placing orders with external suppliers). The outsourcing model is particularly powerful—large MNCs in developed countries place orders with small producers globally. Because MNCs hold market power, they determine prices, quality standards, delivery schedules, and labour conditions. Small producers must accept MNC's terms or lose business.

questions.question: What is liberalisation?
questions.answer: Liberalisation is the removal of government restrictions on trade and investment. When barriers like tariffs and quotas are removed, businesses can freely import/export and foreign companies can invest.
questions.solution: Governments traditionally protected domestic industries through trade barriers: tariffs (taxes on imports) and quotas (limits on import quantities). Liberalisation removes these protections. Starting 1991, India removed barriers to foreign trade and investment. This opened India to competition and globalisation. Liberalisation enables MNCs to operate freely and creates integrated global markets.

questions.question: What is the role of technology in globalisation?
questions.answer: Technology enables globalisation through transportation improvements (containers, air freight) reducing costs, and communication technology (internet, email, satellites) enabling instant global coordination of production.
questions.solution: Container transport revolutionized global shipping by reducing port costs and shipping time. Air transport enabled high-value goods to be shipped globally. Most important is information and communication technology—internet enables instant communication, computers coordinate complex global networks, satellites provide global connectivity. Without these technologies, coordinating production across continents would be impossible. Example: A magazine designed in Delhi, manufactured in India, printed and shipped to London via air, sold globally—all coordinated through technology.

questions.question: What is the World Trade Organization (WTO)?
questions.answer: WTO is an international organization that establishes rules for international trade, ensures countries obey these rules, and pressures countries to remove trade barriers.
questions.solution: WTO was started at the initiative of developed countries to liberalize global trade. About 160 countries are members. WTO's rules require removing trade barriers. However, practice reveals inequities: developed countries retain agricultural subsidies while forcing developing countries to remove support. This unequal application of rules disadvantages developing countries—developed nations protect their farmers while developing country farmers face competition from subsidized imports.

questions.question: Explain with an example how globalisation integrates production across countries.
questions.answer: Production is fragmented globally where different stages occur in different countries. Example: An MNC designs industrial equipment in USA, manufactures components in China (cheap labour), assembles in Mexico, provides customer service in India, sells globally. No single country produces everything; each specializes in where it has advantages.
questions.solution: Modern production is not completed in one factory but spread across multiple countries based on comparative advantages. India has skilled IT workers so customer service is done here. China has cheap labour so manufacturing happens there. USA has technology and capital for research/design. Mexico has trade access to North America for distribution. This fragmentation creates efficiency but also interdependence—if any stage is disrupted, the entire chain breaks. Goods today are truly 'made in the world' rather than 'made in' one country.

questions.question: How does globalisation integrate markets? Use the Chinese toys example.
questions.answer: Globalisation removes trade barriers, allowing goods from any country to enter markets freely. Chinese toys entered Indian markets at lower prices, competing with Indian toys. Within a year, 70-80% of toy shops replaced Indian toys with Chinese. Prices fell for consumers but Indian toy makers faced losses.
questions.solution: When trade barriers are removed, foreign competitors can freely enter domestic markets. Chinese toy manufacturers, operating at lower costs, offered cheaper products to Indian markets. Indian consumers benefited from lower prices and variety. Chinese manufacturers expanded business. But Indian toy makers couldn't compete and faced losses—some went bankrupt. This illustrates market integration's dual effect: consumers benefit while local producers suffer. Over time, Indian economy restructures—toy making might decline while other sectors expand.

questions.question: Who are the beneficiaries of globalisation in India? Give examples.
questions.answer: Beneficiaries: consumers (more choice, lower prices), wealthy sections, educated workers in IT earning high wages, large Indian companies becoming multinationals (Tata Motors, Infosys, Ranbaxy), urban areas.
questions.solution: Globalisation benefits those positioned to take advantage: (1) Consumers access global products—digital cameras, mobiles, cars—at lower prices; (2) Wealthy can afford luxury imports; (3) IT workers earn high wages in booming software industry; (4) Indian companies like Tata Motors and Infosys become global players, creating wealth and employment; (5) Urban areas get infrastructure and job opportunities from MNCs. These benefits concentrate among educated, wealthy, and urban populations—not spread equally.

questions.question: Who are the losers from globalisation? Give examples.
questions.answer: Losers: small producers facing competition from large MNCs; workers in declining sectors facing job losses and wage pressure; rural populations; vulnerable groups lacking skills. Examples: Indian toy makers losing to Chinese competition; Ravi's capacitor unit closing; garment workers earning low wages.
questions.solution: Globalisation harms those lacking competitive advantages: (1) Small producers like Indian toy makers cannot compete with large Chinese manufacturers and close down; (2) Workers in uncompetitive sectors face job losses or wage cuts due to global competition; (3) Rural populations lack market access to global opportunities; (4) Vulnerable groups without skills for high-wage jobs get stuck in low-wage employment; (5) Small farmers struggle when cheap imported agricultural products undercut local production. Example: Ravi's capacitor unit employed 20 workers until import liberalization in 2001; by 2005, production fell and workers lost jobs.

questions.question: Explain how Ravi's capacitor manufacturing unit was affected by globalisation and import liberalisation.
questions.answer: Ravi started a capacitor unit in Tamil Nadu. When India removed import restrictions following WTO agreement (2001), foreign capacitors flooded the market at lower prices. His customers (TV manufacturers) started importing capacitors instead of buying from him. Production fell from full capacity to less than half; many workers lost jobs.
questions.solution: Ravi's case exemplifies globalisation's impact on small producers. His unit thrived under protection—TV manufacturers bought from him because imports were restricted. When barriers were removed, foreign capacitors entered, cheaper due to larger scale and lower labour costs. TV manufacturers switched to imports. Ravi couldn't compete on price—his production fell 50%, workforce reduced. Many similar small units closed entirely. This illustrates how liberalisation, while enabling competition and cheaper products for consumers, destroys small producers lacking economies of scale.

questions.question: What is the difference between production integration and market integration in globalisation?
questions.answer: Production integration: different stages of production occur in different countries based on comparative advantages. Market integration: removing trade barriers allows goods to flow freely between countries, creating global competition and price equalization.
questions.solution: Production integration is the fragmentation of manufacturing—design happens in one country, components manufactured in another, assembly in a third. This creates efficiency through specialization. Market integration occurs when trade barriers are removed—goods produced anywhere compete freely in all markets. Effect on producers differs: production integration allows countries to specialize and develop; market integration exposes domestic producers to global competition. Both are aspects of globalisation but affect economies differently.

questions.question: Why do developed countries' agricultural subsidies harm developing country farmers?
questions.answer: Developed countries subsidize farmers, allowing artificially low prices. Surplus production is sold in developing countries at low prices, undercutting local farmers. Yet WTO rules force developing countries to remove their agricultural support, leaving farmers unprotected.
questions.solution: US agriculture employs 0.5% of population but receives massive government subsidies. This enables American farmers to sell at abnormally low prices. These cheap products flood developing country markets, making local farming unprofitable. Meanwhile, WTO pressures developing countries to cut support to their farmers. Result: developed country farmers protected by subsidies compete with unprotected developing country farmers—unfair competition. Indian farmers face competition from subsidized American agricultural products while losing government support. This exemplifies how WTO rules, claimed to be fair free trade, actually favor developed countries.

questions.question: Analyse how globalisation creates both development opportunities and challenges for India. Use specific sectoral examples.
questions.answer: Opportunities: IT sector boom (Infosys, Wipro) creates high-wage jobs; Tata Motors becomes global player; consumers access global products. Challenges: Small producers (Ravi's unit) face competition; garment workers earn low wages despite export growth; small farmers struggle with imports; rural areas remain marginalized.
questions.solution: Globalisation's impact on India is deeply uneven across sectors and regions. Opportunities concentrate in specific sectors and groups: (1) IT services exploded—India became global software hub, creating high-wage jobs in Bangalore, Pune, Hyderabad; (2) Large manufacturing companies (Tata Motors, Mahindra) expanded globally, becoming multinationals; (3) Consumers benefited from product variety and lower prices. However, challenges affect broad sections: (1) Small manufacturers (like Ravi) couldn't compete with imports after liberalisation; (2) Agriculture faced competition from cheap imports while losing government support; (3) Garment workers in export industries work long hours at low wages for global MNCs; (4) Rural areas without export-oriented industries or IT services remained underdeveloped. Development under globalisation concentrated in urban areas, high-skill sectors, and large companies, while small producers, agricultural workers, and rural populations struggled.

questions.question: Evaluate whether WTO's free trade model is truly 'free and fair' between developed and developing countries. Support with evidence.
questions.answer: WTO's model is neither free nor fair. Developed countries retain protections (agricultural subsidies) while forcing developing countries to remove barriers. Developed countries keep high tariffs on products developing countries export (textiles, agriculture) while expecting market access for their manufactured goods. This asymmetry benefits developed countries and harms developing countries.
questions.solution: WTO claims to promote free and fair trade, but practice reveals systematic bias favoring developed countries: (1) Agricultural subsidies: US, EU heavily subsidize farmers (130+ billion annually), allowing artificially low prices. WTO permits this while forbidding developing country subsidies. Result: developing country farmers face unfair competition. (2) Tariff asymmetry: Developed countries maintain high tariffs on textiles, agriculture (sectors developing countries export) while pushing developing countries to remove barriers to manufactured goods (sectors developed countries excel). (3) Intellectual property: WTO enforces patent protections benefiting pharmaceutical, software companies in developed countries, raising medicine costs in developing countries. (4) Labor standards: WTO doesn't enforce labour standards, allowing developing countries to attract MNCs through low-wage exploitation. (5) Environmental rules: Weak enforcement enables pollution-intensive industries to relocate to developing countries. Evidence: Since WTO's 1995 founding, income gap between developed and developing countries widened, not narrowed, despite promised mutual benefits. Developing countries' share of world trade didn't improve; inequality increased. This shows 'free trade' framework institutionalizes existing power imbalances rather than creating fair competition.

questions.question: Propose comprehensive measures for achieving 'fair globalisation' that benefits all sections of Indian society, not just wealthy and urban areas.
questions.answer: Fair globalisation requires: government protection of workers (labour laws, minimum wages), support for small producers (subsidised credit, technology, market access), fair WTO negotiations, infrastructure expansion reaching rural areas, education and skills training, social protection (health, pensions), technology transfer from MNCs.
questions.solution: Current globalisation benefits few while harming many. Fair globalisation requires comprehensive interventions: (1) Worker protection: Enforce minimum wages, workplace safety, job security even in unorganised sectors; ensure garment workers and casual labourers have dignified working conditions. (2) Small producer support: Provide subsidised credit (unlike formal banks), technology training, market linkages helping small manufacturers compete. (3) Agricultural support: Maintain price supports and subsidies for small farmers facing cheap imports; build supply chains enabling direct sales reducing middlemen. (4) International negotiations: Develop countries must push back against WTO rules favouring developed countries; demand fair agricultural trading terms; protect infant industries during development. (5) Infrastructure expansion: Build roads, electricity, water in rural areas enabling agricultural and small-scale industrial development; connect remote areas to markets. (6) Education and skills: Expand schools and training in rural areas; match training to emerging job opportunities—not all can be IT workers. (7) Social protection: Health insurance, disability coverage, pensions for unorganised sector workers; safety nets enabling risk-taking and investment. (8) Technology transfer: Require MNCs to share technology and train local workers; build domestic capacity for innovation. (9) Local business promotion: Support cooperatives, clusters of small businesses, enabling economies of scale. (10) Environmental sustainability: Ensure globalisation doesn't sacrifice environment through pollution and resource depletion. These measures ensure globalisation benefits all—not just urban, educated, wealthy—enabling inclusive development. Without these, globalisation reinforces inequality.

questions.question: How has globalisation reshaped India's sectoral composition? Analyse the shift from agriculture to services and implications for employment.
questions.answer: Globalisation shifted India toward services, particularly IT and business services. Agriculture's relative importance declined while manufacturing and IT services grew. However, employment hasn't shifted proportionally—agriculture still employs 50%+ while services employ less. This creates underemployment in agriculture and unequal development across sectors and regions.
questions.solution: Globalisation's sectoral impact on India: (1) Agriculture: Faced increasing competition from cheap imports after liberalisation. Government support reduced under WTO pressure. Share in GDP fell, but employment remained high due to land availability and rural population size. Result: agricultural incomes stagnated. (2) Manufacturing: Selected sectors (automobiles, pharmaceuticals, textiles) grew through global integration. But employment didn't expand proportionally due to mechanization. Small manufacturers faced import competition and closed. (3) Services: IT and business services boomed under globalisation. India became global hub for software, business process outsourcing, attracting MNCs and creating high-wage jobs in metros. This created growth but concentrated in urban areas. (4) Regional disparities: Coastal and urban areas with IT/manufacturing hubs developed rapidly. Landlocked agricultural regions fell behind. (5) Employment implications: Globalisation created jobs in IT but not enough to absorb workers from declining agriculture and small manufacturing. Result: many remain underemployed in agriculture; others migrate to cities for low-wage service jobs. Fair globalisation requires: agricultural modernization with social support, manufacturing revival in smaller towns, education enabling workforce transitions, service sector expansion beyond metros. Without these, globalisation-driven development benefits few while most remain trapped in low-productivity agriculture.

questions.question: Compare the impact of globalisation on different social groups in India: wealthy urban consumers, IT professionals, small farmers, garment workers. How does this illustrate globalisation's unequal benefits?
questions.answer: Wealthy urban consumers: gain access to global products, lower prices. IT professionals: high-wage jobs in booming sector. Small farmers: face cheap import competition, reduced government support, declining incomes. Garment workers: employment created but at low wages, poor conditions, job insecurity. Benefits concentrate among wealthy and educated; harms fall on poor and vulnerable.
questions.solution: Globalisation's unequal impact across social groups reveals its contradictory nature: (1) Wealthy urban consumers: Tremendous benefits—access to latest global products (cars, electronics, fashion), lower prices, world-class shopping infrastructure. Their consumption patterns transformed. (2) IT professionals: Major beneficiaries—high-wage jobs (often 5-10x general worker wages), international exposure, career advancement, status. IT boom transformed metros like Bangalore into global tech hubs. (3) Small farmers: Adversely affected—cheap imported agricultural products undercut local production; government reduced support under WTO pressure; incomes fell; many lost land. Climate variability worsens situation. No skill to transition to other sectors. (4) Garment workers: Mixed impact—employment created in export industries when MNCs shifted production to India. But wages are extremely low (Rs 150-200 daily), working conditions harsh, employment insecure (temporary contracts, no benefits), hours long. Female workers especially vulnerable to exploitation. (5) Small manufacturers (like Ravi): Devastated by import competition—couldn't compete with imports after liberalisation; production fell; workers lost jobs. No social safety net. This stratification shows globalisation benefits those already advantaged (wealthy, educated, in urban areas) while harming vulnerable (small producers, rural workers, unorganised sector). Benefits concentrate at top; harms distributed among poor. This inequality perpetuates and worsens under globalisation. Fair globalisation would require redistributing benefits through taxation, support for losers, and ensuring opportunities reach all sections.

questions.question: Examine the role of MNCs in globalisation. Are they agents of development or exploitation? Support with evidence from India.
questions.answer: MNCs drive globalisation but impact is contradictory. Development benefits: Infosys, Tata Motors become global leaders; IT services expand creating high-wage jobs. Exploitation: Garment workers at low wages; technology doesn't transfer to local companies; profits leave India; small producers displaced. MNCs maximise profits, not development.
questions.solution: MNCs are central to globalisation but their impact on developing countries is contradictory, driven by profit motive rather than development: Development contributions: (1) Technology and capital: MNCs bring investment, modern technology, management practices. IT MNCs transformed Indian IT sector into global hub. (2) Employment creation: IT companies employ millions at decent wages; manufacturing MNCs create jobs. (3) Global integration: MNCs enable Indian companies to enter global value chains, gain export experience. (4) Spillover effects: Skilled workers from MNCs start their own ventures; knowledge spreads. Exploitation aspects: (1) Labour exploitation: Garment and manufacturing MNCs source from countries with weak labour laws, enabling low wages and poor conditions. Workers earn Rs 150-200 daily doing work exported as designer goods. Profits to MNCs are huge; worker share minimal. (2) Technology retention: MNCs rarely transfer technology to local companies. Local employees learn but higher-end R&D remains in developed countries. Developing countries remain in low-skill assembly. (3) Profit extraction: Profits earned in India flow back to developed countries; local economy doesn't benefit proportionally. (4) Small producer displacement: MNCs' competition destroys local industries—Ravi's case. No transition support. (5) Environmental damage: Pollution-intensive industries relocate to developing countries; local environment suffers. (6) Dependency creation: Developing countries become dependent on MNC investment; must maintain low wages and weak regulations to attract investment. Conclusion: MNCs aren't inherently developmental or exploitative but serve their profit motive. In pursuit of profit maximization, they often exploit labour and displace local producers. Development benefits exist but are incidental, not intentional. True development requires governments regulating MNCs, protecting workers, supporting local producers, and ensuring benefits are shared rather than concentrated.

questions.question: Critically assess whether India's liberalisation policy (started 1991) was necessary and beneficial overall. What alternative approaches could have achieved development with less disruption to vulnerable groups?
questions.answer: Liberalisation was justified as necessary for competitiveness but created disruption: small producers closed, workers faced wage pressure, farmers struggled. Alternative: gradual, selective liberalisation protecting vulnerable sectors while opening competitive ones; strengthening local industries before exposure to imports; conditional MNC investment with technology transfer and local employment requirements.
questions.solution: Assessment of 1991 liberalisation: Arguments for necessity: (1) India was stagnating under import protection; growth was slow. (2) Protected industries weren't improving efficiency; monopolies existed. (3) Global competition increasingly unavoidable; isolation wasn't sustainable. (4) Foreign investment and technology access required openness. (5) Consumer benefits from competition and product variety. Benefits achieved: (1) Growth accelerated—GDP growth increased from 3-4% to 6-8% post-liberalisation. (2) Technology access—IT services flourished with foreign investment. (3) Consumer benefits—product variety and lower prices. (4) Export opportunities—companies like Tata, Infosys became global. However, costs were severe: (1) Small producers (like Ravi) faced immediate competition and closures. (2) Agricultural sector disrupted by cheap imports. (3) Workers faced wage pressure and job losses. (4) Rural areas and small towns weren't equipped to compete globally. (5) Unequal development—benefits concentrated in metros and select sectors. Alternative approach—gradual, selective liberalisation: (1) Phase opening by sector—open competitive sectors first, protect vulnerable sectors longer. (2) Support transition—provide credit, technology, retraining to small producers instead of sudden exposure. (3) Conditional MNC entry—require technology transfer, local employment, domestic value-addition. (4) Protect agriculture temporarily—maintain support while building competitiveness. (5) Parallel infrastructure—invest in roads, electricity, water in rural areas enabling competition. (6) Education expansion—build workforce skills before exposing to global competition. (7) Gradual approach—reduce barriers slowly, allowing adjustment instead of shock. This would have preserved livelihoods of vulnerable while still achieving opening. Cost would be slightly slower growth but more inclusive development. India chose rapid liberalisation—achieved higher growth but at cost of disruption for vulnerable groups and increased inequality. Whether this trade-off was justified remains debated.
